item.title: One year after USAID’s shutdown, Ethiopian aid workers are still struggling
item.description: The fallout from the aid freeze is still rippling through Ethiopia’s humanitarian workforce, with many laid-off workers unemployed.

item.title: Devex Career Hub: 3 things to know about Africa’s job market in 2026
item.description: This week: What we learned from our digital event on navigating Africa's competitive job market; how communications roles are evolving; featured new job leads; and more.

item.title: Will Africa’s health care reset leave patients footing the bill?
item.description: Africa’s health systems are being forced into a rapid reset as donor funding declines. Governments are raising taxes, borrowing, and cutting services to cope — moves that risk pushing more health care costs onto patients.

item.title: Why human rights defenders say the UK’s quiet policy shift matters
item.description: As attacks on human rights defenders reach record levels, activists in the global south say the U.K. is quietly pulling back the very diplomatic protections that once helped save lives.

item.title: Advice to UN’s Guterres: Call Trump!
item.description: In an interview with Politico last Sunday morning, President Trump discussed the United Nations and expressed some sympathetic thoughts about the organization — after he nearly decimated it last year with his savage cutbacks in American dues and voluntary contributions to the institution, so much so that senior UN officials are now warning that the ... Read more

item.title: Floods and Food Security: The Hidden Cost to Crops and Soil
item.description: South Africa, Mozambique, and Zimbabwe are currently experiencing severe flooding. According to the World Health Organization, 1.3 million people have been affected. In addition, hundreds of people have died , infrastructure has been destroyed, access to health services has been disrupted, and the risks of water- and mosquito-borne diseases are rising. Alarmingly, the devastating impacts […]

item.title: Devex Newswire: Africa gets a much-needed lifeline from Trump
item.description: The U.S. African Growth and Opportunity Act has been revived with a one-year extension signed into law. Plus, U.S. President Donald Trump’s religious revival, and the return of DIV — without USAID.

item.title: Keeping ‘hope alive for younger generations’ in Haiti as funding falters
item.description: Haiti is facing one of the world’s most acute humanitarian crises, driven by escalating gang violence, political paralysis, and deep economic distress.

item.title: Concerns persist over chemical spraying reports on Lebanon’s Blue Line
item.description: The UN reiterated concerns on Friday over reports that Israeli forces sprayed a highly toxic herbicide over areas north of the Blue Line separating Lebanon from Israel on 1 February.

item.title: Guterres welcomes resumption of Iran-US talks
item.description: UN Secretary-General António Guterres on Friday welcomed the resumption of talks between Iran and the United States.

item.title: Ukraine war keeps nuclear safety on a knife-edge, UN watchdog warns
item.description: Attacks on Ukraine’s power system highlight how the ongoing war threatens the safety of the country’s nuclear facilities, the head of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) warned on Friday.

item.title: Pakistan: Guterres condemns deadly suicide bombing at Islamabad mosque
item.description: The UN Secretary-General on Friday strongly condemned an attack on worshippers at a mosque in Pakistan’s capital, Islamabad, after an explosion during prayers reportedly killed dozens and injured many more.

item.title: Nuclear disarmament at breaking point as mistrust grows – but hope remains
item.description: The global system governing nuclear disarmament is facing its most serious crisis in decades, driven by growing mistrust among major powers and the steady erosion of arms control agreements, a senior disarmament expert has warned.

item.title: From shelter to strength: How disaster refuges protect lives in Palau
item.description: The small Pacific Ocean island of Palau is helping communities build resilience against the dangers of climate change-driven extreme weather with the establishment of a network of emergency refuges – thanks to support from the United Nations.

item.title: WHO Launches $1 Billion Appeal Amid Funding Shortfalls and Widening Gaps in Healthcare Access
item.description: On February 3, the World Health Organization (WHO) launched its 2026 global appeal to help millions of people living in protracted conflicts and humanitarian crises access lifesaving healthcare. Following a trend of sharply declining international funding, the agency warns that it is becoming increasingly difficult to respond to emerging health threats, including pandemics and drug-resistant […]

item.title: UN Human Rights Office Launches USD 400 million Appeal to Address Global Human Rights Needs
item.description: The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Türk has launched a USD 400 million funding appeal for 2026 to address global human rights needs, warning that with mounting crises, the world cannot afford a human rights system in crisis. “The cost of our work is low; the human cost of underinvestment is immeasurable,” Türk […]

item.title: UN Security Council: Reform or Irrelevance
item.description: In early January, an emergency UN Security Council meeting on Venezuela followed a familiar path of paralysis. Members clashed over the US government’s abduction of Nicolás Maduro, with many warning it set a dangerous precedent, but no resolution came. This wasn’t exceptional. In 2024, permanent members cast eight vetoes, the highest since 1986. In 2025, […]

item.title: New fund revives USAID’s ‘discovery engine’ outside of government
item.description: USAID’s Development Innovation Ventures served as a rare front door for innovation funding. Now that model moves outside government with the DIV Fund, which will test whether rigorous evidence and flexible capital can still turn small pilots into solutions reaching millions.

item.title: Is the US Planning to Throw a Lifeline to a Sinking UN?
item.description: The current UN financial crisis, described as the worst in the 80-year-old history of the world body, triggers the question: is the US using its financial clout defaulting in its arrears and its assessed contributions to precipitate the collapse of the UN? If the crisis continues, the UN headquarters will be forced to shut down […]

item.title: State Department reboots religious freedom in echo of Trump's first term
item.description: The State Department has announced new funding — and a partnership with Hungary — reminiscent of Trump’s first term.

item.title: Trump signs one-year AGOA extension, preserving US-Africa trade program
item.description: The short-term extension leaves lawmakers little time to negotiate reforms and modernize AGOA.

item.title: The challenges facing philanthropy in 2026
item.description: In a talk with Shelly Helgeson of Connective Impact, we examine how closed funding pipelines, lower risk tolerance, and the loss of USAID are reshaping philanthropy — and why relationship building now matters more than ever.

item.title: Where is HIV prevention drug lenacapavir in ‘America First’ health deals?
item.description: Opinion: Omitting HIV prevention targets from these deals will be devastating to the affordability and scale-up of the drug lenacapavir. African leaders can change that.

item.title: The new power players
item.description: We discuss the new Devex Power 50 list and explore how shifting geopolitical dynamics, the rise of AI, and a growing push for resource sovereignty in Africa are redefining the traditional landscape of global development.

item.title: The post-Axel era at the World Bank
item.description: Axel van Trotsenburg has retired from the bank's second-highest role after nearly four decades. What does his departure mean for the multilateral lender, and what kind of legacy does he leave behind?

item.title: UK aid agency should pause reforms, says parliamentary committee
item.description: Fearing a brain drain, parliamentarians called to pause in-progress reforms to U.K. aid.

item.title: Why ending US food aid to Afghanistan, Yemen threatens national security
item.description: Opinion: The U.S. administration’s justifications to cut all food aid to these two countries are questionable, and create conditions for political instability inconsistent with U.S. interests.

item.title: The skills you need to work in globaldev communications
item.description: Understanding how to reach different audiences and strong judgement in the use of AI are among the skills required.

item.title: Anna Bjerde: Country compacts are a 'game-changer' for World Bank
item.description: Anna Bjerde explains why country-owned compacts, stronger cross-border connectivity along the Lobito Corridor, and water are emerging as core pillars of the bank’s Africa agenda.

item.title: Devex CheckUp: WHO’s next leader might need to be a unicorn
item.description: What kind of leader does the World Health Organization need next? And does that person even exist? Plus, the Gates Foundation doubles down on goals, and a new study reveals that over 200 million people could die due to aid cuts.

item.title: Devex Newswire: Africa’s bid to take charge of its critical minerals
item.description: Inside Africa’s high stakes push for mineral sovereignty; Liberia’s foreign affairs minister tells us why her country is pivoting to economic diplomacy; and how to run a smarter job search in 2026.

item.title: UN lauds $6 billion US funding approval towards ending HIV/AIDS
item.description: The UN agency leading the global effort to end HIV/AIDS worldwide welcomed legislative approval from the United States on Thursday for a $6 billion spending package to help tackle the disease, following nearly a year of sharp aid cuts.

item.title: Cuba: UN warns of possible humanitarian ‘collapse’, as oil supplies dwindle
item.description: The UN on Wednesday warned of potential humanitarian “collapse” in Cuba, following Washington’s attempt to block oil supplies from reaching the island.

item.title: ‘Human rights cannot wait’: Türk launches $400 million appeal for 2026
item.description: UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Türk launched a $400 million appeal on Thursday in Geneva, as his office (OHCHR) continues to operate “in survival mode”.

item.title: Child malnutrition hits catastrophic levels in parts of Sudan
item.description: Acute malnutrition among children has reached catastrophic levels in parts of Sudan’s North Darfur and Greater Kordofan, UN-backed analysts warned on Thursday, as conflict, mass displacement and denials of aid push the country deeper into a famine-risk emergency.

item.title: M23 rebels: UN sees progress in talks but warns violence persists
item.description: The United Nations has welcomed signs of progress in talks aimed at reducing violence in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo.

item.title: UN Police ‘indispensable’ to fulfilling peacekeeping mandates, Security Council hears
item.description: The head of UN Peacekeeping on Thursday called for greater support for international police officers deployed to field missions at a time when financial resources are dwindling and multilateralism is waning.

item.title: Chad: Africa’s refugee haven struggles with its own stark challenges
item.description: Amid the ongoing war in Sudan, Chad, the country receiving the most refugees in Central Africa, saw slight improvements in its humanitarian situation last year, but as one of the most vulnerable nations on the African continent, it is still struggling to support four million people in need.

item.title: World News in Brief: UN condemns attack on civilians in Nigeria, updates from Gaza and West Bank, relief flights to resume in Yemen
item.description: The UN Secretary-General on Thursday strongly condemned a deadly terrorist attack in Kwara state in western Nigeria, where more than 100 people were reportedly killed.

item.title: Fatwas, faith and fallacies: Myth-busting female genital mutilation in Africa
item.description: Olivia Albert, 19, was subjected to female genital (FGM) mutilation when she was 14. She told the UN sexual and reproductive health agency (UNFPA) that she hid her pain because everyone around her said it was normal.

item.title: What kind of leader does WHO need next?
item.description: The World Health Organization director-general elections are taking place in 2027, but expect candidates to come forward this year in the lead-up to the World Health Assembly. Whoever takes up the mantle at the agency will have their work cut out for them.

item.title: Africa’s health security is global security
item.description: Opinion: Africa’s health security is global security. As aid contracts and risks grow, Africa is building sovereign, continent-led systems — proving that resilience, not dependence, is the strategy for a safer world.

item.title: When Protection Meets the Sea: Rethinking Marine Protected Areas with Fishing Communities
item.description: Melanie Brown has been fishing salmon in Bristol Bay, Alaska, for more than 30 years. An Indigenous fisherwoman and a coordinating committee member of the World Forum of Fisher Peoples, she speaks about the sea with deep care and lived knowledge. When interviewed for IPS on Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), a global conservation policy introduced […]

item.title: To Fix the Rupture, Trade is not Enough
item.description: Will trade be enough to navigate the current waves of chaos and disorder that are underpinning the ongoing rifts among competing powerful and hegemon nations and the rest? Amid tectonic shifts in the realm of geopolitics and international relations, amid what the Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney recently defined as a “rupture” in the rules-based […]

item.title: The US foreign aid shock of 2025 gives way to a rapidly evolving 2026
item.description: Devex reporters reflect on the upheaval of last year and where that leaves the development community this year.

item.title: How Africa’s development job market is changing in 2026
item.description: Business models are shifting and there's demand for professionals with problem solving and donor skills. But it's a highly competitive job market and salaries are not what they used to be.

item.title: Devex Dish: The migrant workers who feed the world
item.description: Migrant labor underpins global food production — and in some places, policies are finally starting to catch up. Plus, Washington updates and highlights from Devex’s Power 50 in food and agriculture.

item.title: Devex Newswire: Foreign affairs spending bill becomes law after shutdown
item.description: The approved $50 billion compromise foreign affairs spending bill surpasses the White House's proposals but includes a significant reduction in humanitarian funding compared to previous levels. Plus, a democracy NGO faces down its would-be defunders.

item.title: Welcome to the ‘agrihood’ – the neighbourhood of the future?
item.description: Towns and cities are home to more than half of the world’s population and responsible for around 70 per cent of the greenhouse gas emissions driving the climate crisis, which is why urban planners in Brazil are leading a design revolution that could point the way to creating built-up areas with a dramatically smaller carbon footprint.

item.title: ‘Deepfake abuse is abuse,’ UNICEF warns
item.description: New evidence reveals a proliferation of sexualised images of youngsters generated by artificial intelligence (AI) and a dearth of laws to stop it, the UN Children’s Fund (UNICEF) warned on Wednesday.

item.title: AI ‘moving at the speed of light’ warns Guterres, unveiling recommendations for UN expert panel
item.description: The UN on Wednesday announced the list of experts nominated to the General Assembly to serve on a new Independent International Scientific Panel tasked with assessing how AI is transforming lives worldwide.

item.title: South Sudan: UN forced to suspend food aid after ‘unacceptable’ attacks on convoy
item.description: South Sudan is buckling under a fresh wave of violence and displacement, after attacks and looting halted a major UN food convoy in restive Upper Nile state and clashes continue to spread in neighbouring Jonglei.

item.title: ‘We are dying’: Gaza’s cancer patients plead for a way out
item.description: As World Cancer Day is marked on Wednesday, thousands of patients in Gaza face worsening illness, untreated pain and closed crossings – despite the limited opening of the vital route through Rafah this week.

item.title: Intensifying ISIL threat highlights need to step up counter-terrorism measures
item.description: The terrorist group ISIL continues to adapt and demonstrate resilience despite sustained counter-terrorism efforts, a senior UN official told the Security Council on Wednesday.

item.title: UN chief warns of ‘grave moment’ as final US-Russia nuclear arms treaty expires
item.description: UN Secretary-General António Guterres has warned that the expiration of the New START treaty represents a “grave moment” for international peace and security, as binding limits on US and Russian strategic nuclear weapons fall away amid heightened global tensions.

item.title: To make the business case for gender equality, time for a fresh framing
item.description: Opinion: Changing global realities requires fresh thinking on development finance for gender equality. Updated strategies and coalitions can support new investments.

item.title: ‘We Are Seeing an Economic Transition, but No Democratic Transition’
item.description: CIVICUS discusses the situation following the US intervention in Venezuela with Guillermo Miguelena Palacios, director of the Venezuelan Progressive Institute, a think tank that promotes spaces for dialogue and democratic leadership. On 3 January, a US military intervention culminated in the arrest and extradition of President Nicolás Maduro, who had stayed in power after […]

item.title: Humanitarian Access Collapses as Yemen’s Political and Security Crisis Deepens
item.description: In recent weeks, Yemen’s humanitarian crisis has sharply worsened, as escalating food insecurity and brutal clashes between armed actors have prompted United Nations (UN) officials to warn that the country is approaching a critical breaking point. Intensified violence has increasingly obstructed lifesaving humanitarian operations, while deepening economic and political instability continues to erode access to […]

item.title: Myanmar: Five Years Since the Coup and No End in Sight To War
item.description: Five years of conflict since the military seized power have reduced Myanmar to a failed state and taken a huge toll of lives lost and destroyed. But with all sides seeking total victory, there is no end in sight. Levels of medieval brutality enhanced by modern technology have enabled the military junta, with help from […]

item.title: Conservative push to defund US democracy org falls flat — for now
item.description: The National Endowment for Democracy survived back-to-back defunding efforts by conservative lawmakers to start the year. It still faces an uphill battle to take back the narrative.

item.title: US Congress passes $50 billion foreign affairs bill
item.description: Trump signed the bill, which contains billions in foreign aid funding, into law Tuesday. But there are many questions about what comes next.

item.title: US, Myanmar, Libya, Congo and the 38 Other Countries That Owe the UN Money
item.description: The United States sits atop a list of countries involved in prolonged wars and humanitarian crises that have also not paid their assessed dues on time or at all in the last few years to the United Nations regular operating budget. The nonpayment or late payments have caused such a serious problem that Secretary-General António ... Read more

item.title: Elevating oral health on the global agenda
item.description: Esha Gupta of Colgate-Palmolive and Anil Soni of the WHO Foundation join Devex’s Raj Kumar at Davos for a special edition podcast on the reasons to elevate oral health on the global health agenda.

item.title: Protecting Africa’s Ocean Future and Why a Precautionary Pause on Deep-sea Mining Matters
item.description: The world is entering a decisive period for the future of the ocean. With the High Seas Treaty coming into force and meaningful progress being made on the World Trade Organization Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies, global momentum for stronger marine governance is building. Yet, new pressures linked to the push for deep-sea mining — the […]

item.title: Devex Invested: What happens when a lender and a ratings agency break up?
item.description: The rift between Afreximbank and Fitch exposes a fault line in global finance. Plus, U.S. development finance is scaling up in Africa just as governments push harder on minerals, energy, and industrial policy.

item.title: Support Science in Halting Global Biodiversity Crisis—King Charles
item.description: British Monarch King Charles says science is the solution to protecting nature and halting global biodiversity loss, which is threatening humanity’s survival. In a message to the 12th session of the Plenary of the Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (IPBES), which opened in Manchester, United Kingdom, this week, King Charles said nature […]

item.title: Aid cuts for women and girls leads to instability that reaches us all
item.description: Opinion: A year of aid cuts will lead to generational harm among women and girls and worldwide effects on peace and stability.

item.title: Aid cuts for women and girls lead to instability that reaches us all
item.description: Opinion: A year of aid cuts will lead to generational harm among women and girls and worldwide effects on peace and stability.

item.title: There’s a $660 billion economic opportunity in reproductive health
item.description: Opinion: Bridging the $14B family planning gap requires more than just funding — it takes innovation and cross-sector grit. By scaling digital health and public-private alliances, we can unlock a $660B dividend for humanity.

item.title: Explainer: Why Nature Is Everyone’s Business
item.description: Our food, fuel, and fortunes come from nature, but as these resources are turned into profits, the balance between exploiting and replenishing the planet is ever more precarious. Global businesses impact nature through mining, manufacturing, processing and retail operations. At the same time, nature impacts business operations because there is a loss of biodiversity and […]

item.title: The Delicate Balance of International Migration
item.description: The delicate balance of international migration relies on the high demand for labor and the enforcement of stricter immigration controls. This equilibrium is especially crucial when considering the international migration of students and skilled workers. International students and skilled migrant workers play essential roles in economic development and addressing labor shortages in many countries. However, […]

item.title: Health data governance is an enabler for AI ambitions
item.description: Opinion: The upcoming World Health Assembly can accelerate the responsible use of artificial intelligence in health by grounding action in the ethical and responsible access and use of health data.

item.title: Devex Newswire: What is Gates doubling down on and why?
item.description: Despite substantial reductions in foreign aid, the Gates Foundation remains dedicated to its established priorities. Plus, new figures offer grim predictions about the repercussions of aid cuts.

item.title: Ukraine: Civilians injured, miners killed, in separate Russian attacks
item.description: A fresh wave of Russian strikes overnight across Ukraine injured several people and left thousands “without heat in the heart of winter,” the UN Humanitarian Coordinator in the country said on Tuesday.

item.title: Building healthy bridges towards peace: WHO launches $1 billion appeal
item.description: The World Health Organization (WHO) on Tuesday launched its 2026 global appeal for nearly $1 billion to ensure that millions of people living in humanitarian crises and conflicts can access healthcare.

item.title: Renewed calls for two-State solution amid ‘perilously fragile’ conditions for Palestinians
item.description: The UN committee mandated to promote Palestinian rights opened its 2026 session on Tuesday with renewed calls for a two-State solution, amid a shaky ceasefire in Gaza and continued settler expansion in the West Bank.

item.title: Rafah reopening offers lifeline for patients, but aid flow still far from enough
item.description: The partial reopening of the Rafah crossing between Gaza and Egypt has allowed a small number of people to leave and return to the Strip – including critically ill patients – but the UN has warned that far more is needed for the crossing to function as a genuine humanitarian lifeline.

item.title: Nearly 40 per cent of cancer cases could be prevented, UN study finds
item.description: Up to four in 10 cancer cases globally could be prevented, new analysis has revealed, highlighting the need for stronger tobacco control and other measures to reduce risks and save lives.

item.title: World News in Brief: Children at risk in South Sudan, Balochistan attacks, summit backs boost for undersea cable security
item.description: More than 450,000 children in South Sudan are at risk of acute malnutrition as a surge in violence disrupts health and nutrition services, the UN children’s agency (UNICEF) warned on Tuesday.

item.title: In Washington, Fletcher presses for action as Sudan war grinds on
item.description: Sudan is now the world’s most severe humanitarian emergency, the UN’s top relief official warned on Tuesday, urging donors and diplomatic partners to act swiftly as the fighting nears a third year with no end in sight.

item.title: Gates doubles down on goals in a world weighed down by crisis, CEO says
item.description: Mark Suzman, CEO of the Gates Foundation, tells Devex that amid the "shock" of abrupt aid cuts, the foundation remains focused on its core objectives.

item.title: Is it the Budgetary Crisis – Or Leadership Crisis – Facing the United Nations – Or Both?
item.description: In the month of February 2025, one year ago, United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres commenced his briefing of the media by announcing that “I want to start by expressing my deep concern about information received in the last 48 hours by UN agencies — as well as many humanitarian and development NGOs — regarding severe […]

item.title: High Seas Treaty Will Transform Our Fragile Ocean for the Better
item.description: “The ocean’s health is humanity’s health”, said UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres, in September 2025. He was commenting after the High Seas Treaty (BBNJ) [1] finally achieved ratification, going on to call for “a swift, full implementation” from all partners. As of January 17, 2026, the treaty has come into force, meaning the time for implementation […]

item.title: Group of 77—Representing 134 Nations, Plus China– Protest Funding Cuts for South-South Cooperation
item.description: A sharp cut in funding for “South-South Cooperation” (UNOSSC) has triggered a strong protest from the 134-member Group of 77 (G-77), described as the largest intergovernmental organization of developing countries within the United Nations. The protest has been reinforced by four UN ambassadors, two of them former chairs of the G77—Colombia (1993) and South Africa […]

item.title: Aid cuts could lead to millions of deaths by decade’s end, new study finds
item.description: A new study estimates that by the end of the decade, between 9.4 million and 22.6 million people could die as a result of aid cuts across the world.

item.title: Former Chilean leader Michelle Bachelet enters race to lead UN
item.description: During Trump's first term, the U.S. and Israel sought unsuccessfully to derail her campaign for U.N. high commissioner for human rights.

item.title: Michelle Bachelet of Chile Is Running for the UN’s Top Post With Regional Backing
item.description: Chilean President Gabriel Boric has officially submitted the nomination of former President Michelle Bachelet to be a candidate in the race for the next secretary-general of the United Nations, succeeding António Guterres, a Portuguese politician who has led the organization since 2017. Boric made the announcement on Feb. 2, saying that the required documents have ... Read more

item.title: Venezuela at a Crossroads
item.description: When US special forces seized Nicolás Maduro and his wife from the presidential residence in Caracas on 3 January, killing at least 24 Venezuelan security officers and 32 Cuban intelligence operatives in the process, many in the Venezuelan opposition briefly dared hope. They speculated that intervention might finally bring the democratic transition thwarted when Maduro […]

item.title: Money Matters: The Gates Foundation spent $4.5B last year — who got it?
item.description: An analysis of the Gates Foundation’s top grantees in 2025. Plus, a new U.S. State Department strategy, and the risk of “imminent financial collapse” at the U.N.

item.title: Decolonization language risks alienating the public, UK MPs warned
item.description: A parliamentary hearing saw frank discussion of the aid sector's communication challenges.

item.title: Devex Newswire: The 50 powerful people you need to know
item.description: We've made a list of people who are remaking the development sector. Plus, a battle rages at the U.S. Institute of Peace, and the U.N. chief warns that the world body could face "imminent financial collapse" due to unpaid member dues.

item.title: Invisible highways: The vast network of undersea cables powering our connectivity
item.description: Every day, we send countless emails, take part in video calls, use search engines and streaming services, while seamlessly banking online.

item.title: Gaza: Limited Rafah crossing reopening sparks hope – but also ‘massive trepidation’
item.description: The reopening of the Rafah crossing in the southern Gaza Strip on Monday after more than a year is being met with both optimism and fear, a senior official with the UN agency that assists the Palestinian people, UNRWA, has said.

item.title: Global health systems ‘at risk’ as funding cuts bite, warns WHO
item.description: The UN World Health Organization (WHO) warned on Monday that cuts to international aid and persistent funding gaps are undermining the global health system.

item.title: UN peacekeeping patrols suspended along Lebanon-Israel Blue Line
item.description: UN peacekeepers temporarily suspended patrols and other activities along parts of the ‘Blue Line’ in southern Lebanon after the Israeli military said it would release what it described as a non-toxic chemical substance near the frontier, the UN said on Monday.

item.title: Devex Power 50
item.description: Who is shaping this historic new era of development? We reveal the people you need to know.

item.title: Devex monthly news quiz: January 2026
item.description: News from Davos usually takes the spotlight in globaldev during January. But this year, that spotlight is shared with Trump — again. With those dominating the airwaves, what other big news could you have missed? Get a recap through our quiz!

item.title: To Develop a Continent, Africa Must Nourish Its Children
item.description: Hunger shadowed Mercy Lung’aho’s childhood, fueling her campaign to promote nutrition as a foundation for Africa’s development. As lead for the Food Security, Nutrition and Health Program at the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA), this certified nutritionist and researcher, with more than 20 years of championing development, is advocating for an integrated approach combining […]

item.title: Do Resources Define the Parameters of Faith-based Engagement and Diplomacy Today?
item.description: Several events, meetings, consultations, initiatives, etc. taking place among faith-inspired, ‘faith-based’ and a variety of other similar efforts, over the past year, in the United States especially, concern me. Coming from a background of human rights, international development, and humanitarian service, I have witnessed the arc of ‘none’ to increasing interest by Western governments in […]

item.title: Devex Pro Insider: What comes next for US foreign assistance? Don't ask the experts
item.description: Officials are looking for experts to complete the shutdown of USAID — but former employees are excluded. Plus, the Peace Corps has a new acting director.

item.title: The Right Way to Stop a New Nuclear Arms Race
item.description: We have entered 2026 with the highest threat ever from nuclear weapons, and we are on the cusp of the expiration of the last remaining arms control agreement between the United States and Russia, the New START treaty, removing the last restraints on an accelerating arms race. This dangerous moment comes on Feb. 5, as ... Read more

item.title: From Cape Town to London, Deo Kato runs to challenge racism and reclaim the migration narrative
item.description: Step by step, mile by mile, Deo Kato ran his way across a continent — and beyond. After a year and a half on the road, the Ugandan British runner and campaigner has become the first person to run from Cape Town to London.

item.title: Can workers compete with machines and stay relevant in the AI era?
item.description: AI looks set to be transformative for us all, but it also brings a real risk of job losses and widening social and economic divides. UN experts are focusing on how to manage that transition, to ensure the benefits of the technology outweigh the threats.

item.title: [UN: Jan. 30] Spain Calls for a Woman to Lead UN, Guterres’s Stark Warning & Lots More
item.description: This Week @UN: Trump to UN: Drop Dead; a female/feminist SecGen; a UN-merger risk. Plus: Gaza/West Bank/Israel; Holocaust remembrance; UN-Haiti updates; Guterres’s priorities in 2026; UN death spiral. Follow us in real time on Blue Sky, Twitter (X), Facebook, Instagram and LinkedIn • Our #1 story: UN Pension Review Raises Concerns Among Staff and Retirees, op-ed ... Read more

item.title: US funding pledge insufficient to avert UN financial woes
item.description: U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres warns U.N. risks "imminent financial collapse."

item.title: U.S. Exit from Paris Agreement Deepens Climate Vulnerability for the Rest of the World
item.description: On January 27, the United States officially withdrew from the Paris Agreement, an international treaty adopted in 2015 aiming to reduce global warming and strengthen countries’ resilience to climate impacts. Following a year of regulatory rollbacks and sustained efforts by the Trump administration to dismantle federal climate policy, this move is expected to trigger wide […]

item.title: Afreximbank cuts ties with Fitch, exposing a fault line in global finance
item.description: The break highlights growing tensions over how African institutions are rated — and who gets to decide what counts as a preferred creditor.

item.title: Devex Career Hub: The top-paying roles for skilled ICT pros
item.description: This week: salary and hiring trends that ICT professionals need to know; how development academia has changed post-USAID; featured new job leads, and more.

item.title: In Mozambique, conservation meets care in a bold health experiment
item.description: A new plan aims to build a hospital and health campus in rural Mozambique, offering specialized care not yet available in the region. Project partners hope that it can serve as a model for rural health care delivery in Africa.

item.title: Devex Newswire: USAID hires contractors — but bars ex-USAID staff
item.description: In a memo, the officials overseeing the shutdown of what remains of USAID explain the rationale behind the ban, saying: “By recruiting a fresh workforce, the Agency aims to avoid the risk of impaired objectivity.” Plus, is the U.K.’s aid watchdog at risk?
